mnemonics: Static Stays Single: A static member belongs to the class itself, not to any single instance. There's only one copy, shared by all objects.
Class Coat: Imagine a 'static' variable or method as a coat worn by the class itself, not by the individual objects (instances) created from the class.
No 'this' for Static: Static methods cannot use the 'this' keyword because 'this' refers to the current instance, and static methods aren't tied to an instance.
keyPoints: Static variables (class variables) are shared among all instances of a class. Changing its value in one object changes it for all.
Static methods can be called without creating an instance of the class. They are called using the class name (e.g., `ClassName.staticMethod()`).
Static methods can only directly access other static members (variables and methods) of the class; they cannot access instance members directly.
A common use of static is for utility methods (like `Math.sqrt()`) or constants (like `Math.PI`).
The `main` method in Java is static because it must be called by the JVM before any objects are created.
commonMistakes: Trying to access non-static (instance) members directly from a static method, which causes a compilation error.
Assuming that each object gets its own copy of a static variable, when in reality all instances share the same single copy.
Overusing the static keyword, which can lead to code that is difficult to test and not object-oriented.
